content.text: The Role of Review Rituals in Error Budget Management

Error budget numbers sitting in a Grafana dashboard are inert. They become actionable only when the right people see them together and make decisions from them. The monthly review meeting is where SRE and product sit in the same room, look at the same budget graph, and negotiate the velocity-reliability trade-off explicitly rather than letting it happen implicitly through individual deploy decisions.

Without a regular review ritual, two failure modes emerge. The first is the SRE team burning budget quietly managing freezes and exceptions, while product is unaware of the reliability cost of their feature velocity. The second is product over-indexing on reliability (because they have heard of the freeze policy) and unnecessarily slowing velocity when the budget is actually healthy. The review ritual is the information exchange that prevents both.

attrs.alt: Error budget review cadence: weekly standup, monthly review, quarterly SLO calibration, and incident-triggered review with standard monthly agenda

content.text: A complete error budget review program uses four cadences at different time scales, each with different participants and scope.
Freeze risk for the week; any planned deploys reviewed
Burn attribution, velocity/reliability balance, action items
SLO target calibration, freeze policy updates, reliability investment for next quarter
SRE on-call + lead, feature owner, PM
Root cause of burn event, freeze policy adherence review, action items

For GenAI services: add the ML engineer or model owner to all four cadences. Eval quality and safety SLO burns are often caused by model or prompt changes, and those root causes are not visible to the SRE team without ML context.

The weekly standup is not a meeting — it is a 15-minute check-in with a fixed agenda that never expands. Its job is to prevent surprises during the week. If the budget is healthy, the standup is 5 minutes. If a burn event is developing, it is 15 minutes and produces a Jira ticket for investigation.
Current budget remaining for each SLO (read from the live dashboard, no slides)
Any active burn rate alerts from the past 7 days
Deploys planned for the coming week that carry reliability risk
Current freeze status — are any SLOs in soft freeze risk territory

The standup should be async-first when the budget is healthy: post the dashboard link to Slack with a 'no issues' note and skip the synchronous call. Reserve the synchronous call for weeks when a burn alert fired or a high-risk deploy is planned.

The monthly review is the core ritual. It is the meeting where product managers learn what their feature deploys cost in reliability terms, and where SRE learns what upcoming features will require in terms of reliability investment. Both parties leave with the same picture of the budget.
Send the pre-read to Slack 24 hours before the meeting. The pre-read is generated automatically from Prometheus so that meeting time is spent on decisions, not on pulling numbers.

# budget_report.py  — generates monthly review pre-read
from datetime import date, timedelta
import requests
import os

PROMETHEUS_URL = os.environ['PROMETHEUS_URL']
SLACK_WEBHOOK  = os.environ['SLACK_REVIEW_WEBHOOK']

SLO_IDS = [
    ('availability',  'Availability'),
    ('latency_p95',   'Latency P95'),
    ('eval_quality',  'Eval Quality'),
    ('safety_flag',   'Safety Flag Rate'),
]

def query(metric: str) -> float:
    resp = requests.get(
        f'{PROMETHEUS_URL}/api/v1/query',
        params={'query': metric},
        timeout=5,
    )
    result = resp.json()['data']['result']
    return float(result[0]['value'][1]) if result else float('nan')

def build_report() -> str:
    today = date.today()
    reset = today.replace(day=1) + timedelta(days=32)
    reset = reset.replace(day=1)
    days_left = (reset - today).days

    lines = [
        f'*Monthly Error Budget Review — {today.strftime("%B %Y")}*',
        f'Budget window resets in {days_left} days\n',
        '*Budget Remaining by SLO:*',
    ]

    for slo_id, label in SLO_IDS:
        remaining = query(
            f'slo:error_budget:remaining{{slo_id="{slo_id}"}}'
        )
        burn_rate = query(
            f'slo:error_budget:burn_rate1h{{slo_id="{slo_id}"}}'
        )
        days_until_exhausted = (
            remaining / burn_rate * 30
            if burn_rate > 0 else float('inf')
        )

        if remaining > 0.20:
            status = ':white_check_mark: Normal'
        elif remaining > 0.05:
            status = ':warning: Soft Freeze Risk'
        else:
            status = ':no_entry: Hard Freeze Risk'

        lines.append(
            f'  • {label}: {remaining:.1%} remaining | '
            f'burn {burn_rate:.1f}x | '
            f'exhausts in {days_until_exhausted:.0f}d | '
            f'{status}'
        )

    lines += [
        '',
        '*Agenda:* https://wiki/error-budget-monthly-agenda',
        '*Dashboard:* https://grafana/error-budget-overview',
    ]
    return '\n'.join(lines)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    report = build_report()
    requests.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK, json={'text': report}, timeout=5)
    print('Pre-read posted to Slack')
Run the monthly review in five sections with strict time boxes. The facilitator is the SRE lead; decisions are owned by the EM and PM jointly.
Budget Scorecard (10 min): current remaining per SLO, 30-day trend, days until reset, freeze risk for this month
Burn Attribution (15 min): which events consumed budget, what caused each, which team owns it
Velocity vs Reliability (15 min): features shipped versus budget consumed, is the trade-off acceptable, upcoming high-risk releases
Action Items (10 min): open reliability work with owners and due dates
Next Month Forecast (10 min): projected remaining at window end if burn rate holds, freeze risk, features to defer if budget tightens
Burn Attribution: Connecting Budget to Engineering Decisions

Attribution is the most technically demanding part of the monthly review. The goal is to connect each budget burn event to the engineering action that caused it, so that the conversation is concrete: 'the eval quality SLO lost 12% of its monthly budget when the summarization prompt was updated on the 14th, owned by the content team' is actionable. 'Eval quality is at 68%' is not.

# burn_attribution.py  — links budget burn events to deploys
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class BurnEvent:
    start: datetime
    end: datetime
    slo_id: str
    budget_consumed: float  # fraction of 30-day budget
    cause: str              # 'deploy', 'provider', 'infra', 'unknown'
    owner_team: Optional[str] = None
    deploy_sha: Optional[str] = None

def attribute_burn_events(
    burn_events: list[BurnEvent],
    deploys: list[dict],   # [{sha, team, timestamp}]
) -> list[BurnEvent]:
    """
    Heuristic: if a burn event starts within 30 minutes after a deploy,
    attribute it to that deploy and team.
    """
    attributed = []
    for event in burn_events:
        matched_deploy = None
        for deploy in deploys:
            delta = (event.start - deploy['timestamp']).total_seconds()
            if 0 <= delta <= 1800:  # within 30 minutes after deploy
                matched_deploy = deploy
                break
        if matched_deploy:
            event.cause = 'deploy'
            event.owner_team = matched_deploy['team']
            event.deploy_sha = matched_deploy['sha']
        attributed.append(event)
    return attributed

def summarize_by_team(
    events: list[BurnEvent],
) -> dict[str, float]:
    """Total budget consumed per team this window."""
    totals: dict[str, float] = {}
    for e in events:
        key = e.owner_team or e.cause
        totals[key] = totals.get(key, 0.0) + e.budget_consumed
    return dict(sorted(totals.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1]))

Attribution categories for GenAI systems go beyond the usual deploy/infra/provider split. Add prompt changes, model version upgrades, and eval harness changes as distinct categories. Each has a different owner team and a different mitigation path.
New code path increased error rate or latency
Updated system prompt shifted model behavior
Provider model update changed latency or quality distribution
LLM API provider outage or degradation
Retry logic, fallback model, circuit breaker
Eval test set or scoring changed, not model behavior
Recalibrate eval baseline; not a real quality regression

The five-minute 'next month forecast' section requires a forward-looking estimate. At the current burn rate, how much budget will remain at the end of the 30-day window? If the answer is below 20%, the team should discuss what to defer.

Present three scenarios at the monthly review: optimistic (burn rate drops to 1x), current (burn rate holds), and pessimistic (burn rate increases by 50%). This gives the PM a range to plan against rather than a single projection that will not hold.
Standardize the output of each monthly review using a shared template. This makes the review searchable over time, reveals patterns across months, and gives new team members context.

# Confluence / Notion template — Monthly Error Budget Review
#
# Copy this template for each review session.
# Fill from the pre-read dashboard before the meeting starts.

## Date: [YYYY-MM-DD]
## Attendees: [names]

### 1. Budget Scorecard
| SLO            | Remaining | 30-day Trend | Burn Rate | Freeze Risk |
|----------------|-----------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| Availability   |           |              |           |             |
| Latency P95    |           |              |           |             |
| Eval Quality   |           |              |           |             |
| Safety Rate    |           |              |           |             |

### 2. Burn Attribution
| Date | Event | Caused By | Team | Budget Consumed |
|------|-------|-----------|------|-----------------|
|      |       |           |      |                 |

### 3. Velocity vs Reliability
- Features shipped this month: [N]
- Budget spent on feature-caused burns: [%]
- Is this trade-off acceptable? [Y/N, notes]
- High-risk releases planned next month: [list]

### 4. Action Items
| Action | Owner | Due | Priority |
|--------|-------|-----|----------|
|        |       |     |          |

### 5. Next Month Forecast
- Projected budget remaining at window end (if burn rate holds): [%]
- Freeze risk: [Low / Medium / High]
- Features to defer if budget tightens: [list]

The quarterly review is the governance layer. It asks whether the SLO targets themselves are still correctly calibrated — not just whether the system is meeting them. This is where the conversation shifts from operational management to policy design.
SLO target calibration: were the targets tight enough to catch real user pain, or did teams spend budget without users noticing?
Freeze policy review: were freeze thresholds set at the right levels? Did freezes trigger too early or too late?
Reliability investment planning: what engineering work in the next quarter will reduce burn rate? Which SLOs need more headroom?
GenAI-specific: do eval quality SLOs reflect what users actually care about, or are they measuring proxy metrics that have drifted from user satisfaction?

Quarterly reviews should produce explicit decisions, not just discussions. If the SLO target is being changed, write down the old value, new value, and rationale. If the freeze threshold is being adjusted, record why. These decisions become the institutional memory of your reliability program.
Product and EM rarely attend; decisions are made without business context
Block the monthly review on PM and EM calendars as required; escalate absences to director level
Review discusses percentages with no connection to specific events or teams
Require burn attribution table as pre-read before meeting starts; no attribution = meeting is rescheduled
Review only covers what happened, never what is about to happen
Make next-month forecast the last agenda item; PM must leave with a freeze risk assessment for their roadmap
Meeting produces discussion but no written action items with owners
Use the meeting notes template; post action items to team Slack channel within 1 hour of meeting end
Eval quality burns are attributed to the model provider and not investigated further
Require ML engineer attendance; distinguish provider degradation from internal prompt/eval drift with data

Error budget review rituals convert budget numbers into shared organizational awareness and decisions. Four cadences — weekly standup, monthly review, quarterly SLO review, and incident-triggered review — cover the full range from tactical day-to-day monitoring to strategic target calibration. The monthly review is the center of gravity: automate the pre-read, run a structured five-part agenda, require burn attribution to connect budget events to engineering decisions, and record every action item with an owner. For GenAI systems, include the ML engineer in all cadences and explicitly distinguish prompt, model, and eval harness changes as budget attribution categories.
